channel.title: Athan Dial on Athan Dial - Portfolio
channel.description: Recent content in Athan Dial on Athan Dial - Portfolio

item.title: AI Adoption Needs a Measurement Layer
item.description:
Context

Leadership wanted the leverage of applied AI across the company, but uptake was uneven and unmeasured—strong in a few pockets and absent in others. If we let adoption spread ad hoc, we would pay for tools without learning where they helped, where they hurt, and what should be built next.

The problem was not access to AI tools; it was the absence of a repeatable adoption system. Some teams had integrated ChatGPT into daily workflows, others had not touched any AI tool, and there was no shared frame for what “good usage” looked like. Without an evaluation model, every decision defaulted to vibes: feature lists, vendor demos, and the loudest internal advocates. That produced a second-order risk: adoption would happen in pockets, but the organization would not get better at using these tools over time because the learning never accumulated.

item.title: Competitive Intelligence Runs on Identity
item.description:
Context

Leadership needed a live map of 15–20 competitor programs to steer pipeline bets, but the closest thing we had was a scientist eyeballing Excel diffs once a week. Shipping a competitive intelligence platform in three weeks meant proving that entity resolution—not visualization—was the blocker, and that the tradeoff becomes obvious instead of theoretical when we invested in canonical identities first.

The inherited workflow compared last week’s spreadsheet to this week’s and hoped nothing important changed names. “Pfizer Inc.”, “Pfizer, Inc.”, and “Pfizer Global R&D” were different rows, clinical trials showed up twice with slightly different metadata, and once a diff was done the prior state disappeared forever. We needed to watch organizations, assets, and biological targets across AACT, ChEMBL, Open Targets, EFO, and ROR, but every source disagreed on identifiers.

item.title: Data Quality Is an Incident Discipline
item.description:
Context

A single misspelled configuration field quietly pushed six FDA-approved compounds through three discovery programs and wasted about $90, but the real damage was the doubt it sowed in a pipeline that controls six-figure purchasing decisions. The production data platform powering dashboards, nomination tools, and leadership reviews ingested external sources, applied layered transformations, and surfaced boolean flags that dictated procurement. The underlying problem: data quality failures in applied AI workflows rarely explode loudly. They produce plausible numbers that lie quietly until a scientist notices a weird compound on a list months later. Without structured incident response, those errors erode trust faster than they burn dollars.

item.title: Decision Intelligence Needs Structure and Nerve
item.description:
Context

Every compound selection round committed tens of thousands of dollars and months of lab time, yet six months later no one could answer “why these molecules?” The discovery programs generated assay hits, fail records, procurement events, and narrative briefs scattered across tools. None of it rolled up into a traceable decision log. Dashboards showed pass counts without fail counts, leaders asked for enrichment deltas we could not justify, and analyses vanished inside personal notebooks. The problem threatened to lock the next nomination cycle into high-dollar bets built on incomplete or biased data.

item.title: Influence Comes From Decision Infrastructure
item.description:
Context

I took a reactive data work team and made it a strategic partner in drug discovery investment decisions over 18 months. The cost of staying in “service desk” mode was decision-integrity risk, burnout, and missed portfolio leverage: we were informing choices worth tens of thousands of dollars per round plus months of downstream lab work without any seat at the table.

The inherited operating model was simple: stakeholders sent ad-hoc requests (“pull hit rates for this target,” “how many compounds passed the filter?”) and we delivered fast, accurate outputs. That responsiveness created three compounding failure modes.

item.title: Product Signals for Scientific Tools
item.description:
Context

Internal discovery tools decide which experiments get prioritized, yet they were being specced in Slack and launched without adoption plans. Installing product discipline meant scientists stopped guessing whether a tool worked and leadership could see the tradeoff becomes obvious instead of theoretical when two platforms competed for the same quarter.

The tooling backlog spanned similarity search, compound analytics, cross-reference dashboards, and data reporting. None had requirements, personas, or success metrics. Whoever yelled loudest got engineering cycles, and the only governance was “ship before the OKR checkpoint.”

item.title: Similarity Decisions Need Weighted Frameworks
item.description:
Context

Chemists needed similarity results under 60 seconds to support a 22-requirement PRD and an immovable Phase 0 milestone. The backend debate was drifting toward vendor demos and gut feel. Choosing between Postgres+RDKit or Milvus on Kubernetes without structure risked burning a sprint while still producing an argument with no clear resolution.

Ownership

I owned the decision architecture and stakeholder alignment. I built the weighted framework, walked chemists and platform engineers through the rationale, and designed the API abstraction that would preserve optionality regardless of which backend won.

item.title: Training Data as Product: Building a Reusable Dataset Pipeline
item.description:
Context

Our cyclic peptide program had the modeling stack in place, but lacked the training data to run it. The modeling team was standing by, but no applied AI training dataset existed for this therapeutic protein target. Public bioactivity coverage was sparse, and the obvious starting point—ChEMBL—didn’t have sufficient relevant data.

The richest signal source was a published patent: dozens of pages of compound tables, activity annotations, and structural descriptions. Extracting that into usable training data meant making judgment calls that affect downstream behavior: what counts as a positive, what as a negative, how to represent structure, how to manage class imbalance, and how to preserve provenance so results survive scientific review during nomination.

item.title: Warehouse Discipline Without a Data Team
item.description:
Context

The compound nomination engine and executive dashboards were hanging off raw Athena tables, so every mis-joined column risked steering six-figure wet-lab schedules. Advanced applied AI experiments were running on top of ad-hoc SQL. Analysts stitched assay results, procurement logs, and vendor catalogs together differently every week, so an enrichment chart was a suggestion rather than a fact. Modeling peers called out that a single typo in those long queries could swing nomination priorities, yet no one owned a transformation layer. Without lineage, knowledge lives in a system or in someone’s head, so regression analysis, procurement automation, and leadership reports kept contradicting each other. Every compound-level question turned into a Slack thread plus a copied SQL snippet that no one trusted.

item.title: The Shadow Process Problem: Systematizing a Scientific Workflow
item.description:
Context

A biology collaborator was doing mechanism-of-action analysis by pasting experimental data into ChatGPT sessions and manually curating outputs in Excel. She had real judgment about what mattered and what to filter. The process worked — for one person, on one dataset, with no audit trail.

A second collaboration was incoming. Different dataset, different scientists. The manual workflow had a clear ceiling, and we were about to hit it.

Ownership

I co-designed the replacement workflow with the biologist. My role: run a structured intake session, document what she was already doing precisely enough to formalize it, and build a pipeline that produced output she could react to.

item.title: 100+ Internal Tools and No Way to Know Which Ones Matter
item.description:
Context

Over 100 internal software tools were imported into an application catalog. The catalog had a comprehensive list of what existed. It didn’t have a useful answer to the harder question: which of these, if they stopped working tomorrow, would cause a real problem?

Each tool was tagged with a lifecycle status — active or inactive. A leadership presentation was due in four days. I had until then to turn a flat list of apps into something that could actually guide governance decisions.

item.title: About
item.description:
Athan Dial
01 // Background

I lead the design of decision systems and applied AI inside scientific organizations. The day job is Associate Director of Data Science & Product Management at Montai Therapeutics. The training is a PhD in multi-omics analysis.

The path from bench science to product wasn’t planned. The transferable skill turned out to be designing decision systems under uncertainty — figuring out what counts as evidence, which experiment is worth the cost, and how to keep a team’s confidence calibrated when truth comes back late or partial. PhD work taught those moves; drug discovery ML uses them every day.

item.title: Decision Portfolio
item.description:
Download PDF resume

Athan Dial, PhD

PhD-trained in multi-omics analysis. Product-tested in drug discovery ML.

I design decision systems for ambiguous, high-stakes problems. I combine research-grade evaluation rigor with product judgment to ship measurable outcomes. I communicate in decisions, metrics, and tradeoffs—not activity.

Decision Systems Portfolio

System 1: Preventing Metric Theater in Drug Discovery ML

Decision Context: Data science teams were presenting accuracy metrics without reliability monitoring, creating false confidence in model predictions for multimillion-dollar compound selection decisions. Each compound decision represented $2M+ in development costs, but we lacked visibility into model performance degradation over time.

item.title: Talks & Conversations
item.description:
I write and speak about decision systems, evaluation under uncertainty, and how scientific organizations actually adopt AI — beyond the demo, into the operating layer.

Most of my time goes into the day job. The rest I spend trading notes with peers in data science, product, and research leadership who are working through similar problems.

What I’m thinking about

These are the conversations I don’t get tired of:

  • Evaluation when ground truth is delayed, partial, or contested. What counts as evidence, and what’s metric theater dressed up as rigor.
  • Decision-support tooling that scientists actually use. Instead of bypass with a side of resentment.
  • Applied AI inside scientific organizations. The models are the easy part — the operating layer is where it lives or dies.
  • The translation layer. Research rigor on one side, product judgment on the other, and the people in between trying to ship.

If your team is working through any of these and would benefit from an outside perspective — for a talk, a panel, a guest session, or a longer conversation — drop a note.

item.title: Writing
item.description:
Right now, most of my writing lives in my case studies. They’re written as decision narratives: context → frame → tradeoffs → outcome.

  • Decision systems case studies
  • RSS feed: Case studies RSS

If you’re looking for something specific, start with:

  • Preventing Metric Theater in Drug Discovery ML
  • Reducing Pipeline Latency to Enable Real-Time Decision Loops

item.title: Reducing Data Pipeline Latency to Enable Real-Time Decision Loops
item.description:
Context

Business teams were making critical decisions on data that was 2-3 days old, causing them to miss market opportunities and react to problems rather than prevent them. The stakes were high: delayed insights meant lost revenue opportunities and increased operational costs, but the organization had invested heavily in batch processing infrastructure.

This work lived in the core data platform that served analytics teams, operations teams, and executive dashboards across the organization. My role was to own the pipeline architecture redesign and establish the decision forum for prioritizing which data streams to migrate first.

item.title: Preventing Metric Theater in Drug Discovery ML
item.description:
Context

Data science teams were presenting accuracy metrics without reliability monitoring, creating false confidence in model predictions for high-stakes compound selection decisions. The stakes were high: each compound decision carried significant development costs, but teams lacked visibility into model performance degradation over time.

This work lived in the drug discovery ML platform, where prediction models guide which compounds advance to expensive in-vitro and in-vivo testing phases. My role was to own the evaluation framework design and establish decision forums with clear ownership.

item.title: Build vs Buy: Strategic Analysis for Analog Generation
item.description:
Context

Late 2025 brought Montai to a strategic crossroads. Our core IP hinged on generating novel analog compounds through proprietary AI models — but the internal generative model produced a large library of virtual compounds with uncertain synthetic feasibility. Meanwhile, an external AI partner offered curated, higher-confidence AI-suggested compounds.

Facts:

  • Late 2025: Montai’s core IP = generating novel analog compounds via proprietary AI
  • Problem: Internal generative model produced many virtual compounds, but many not synthesizable or of uncertain value
  • Alternative: An external AI partner offered curated compounds (more drug-like, higher confidence)
  • Stakes: Resource allocation — invest in internal model improvement OR buy external suggestions?

The CSO needed an evidence-based recommendation by December 1, 2025. This wasn’t a philosophical debate about build vs buy — it was a portfolio allocation decision with measurable ROI implications. I had three weeks to model the tradeoffs quantitatively and make a clear recommendation.

item.title: Learning Agendas: Bringing Research Rigor to Product Decisions
item.description:
Context

By 2025, Montai ran multiple concurrent R&D experiments — AI model iterations, assay validations, Anthrolog generation improvements. Each experiment had implicit goals but lacked explicit success criteria. The result: debates about “when to pivot” and “when to scale” became opinion-driven rather than evidence-backed.

Facts:

  • By 2025: Multiple concurrent experiments (AI models, assays, analog generation improvements)
  • Problem: Unclear success criteria per experiment (when to pivot? when to scale?)
  • Example confusion: “AI model improved accuracy” but didn’t translate to better compound selection
  • Stakes: Wasted months on meandering experiments without clear learning goals

The core issue traced back to a fundamental principle from my PhD training: experiments without pre-defined hypotheses produce data, not learning. In academic research, you write your aims before running experiments. In biotech R&D, we were running experiments and retroactively deciding whether results “felt good enough.” This had to change.

item.title: From Data Crisis to Data Culture: A Critical Program Incident
item.description:
Context

Mid-2025 was a period of rapid growth at Montai — more programs, more models, more data flowing through pipelines built for smaller scale. Technical debt had accumulated in migration work from earlier systems, creating latent risks that hadn’t yet manifested. Until one critical program.

Facts:

  • Mid-2025: A critical discovery program found predictions missing from the warehouse
  • Impact: Could not evaluate nominations for the affected program (significant program investment at stake)
  • Symptom: Our program monitoring dashboard failed, analysis queries returned incomplete results
  • Urgency: Program decisions on hold, stakeholder trust eroding

The stakes extended beyond the immediate technical bug. This was organizational credibility on the line — scientists needed confidence that data infrastructure wouldn’t become a bottleneck to discovery. A 6-week delay on a critical program signaled deeper quality issues, and stakeholders rightfully questioned whether other datasets harbored similar problems.

item.title: Standardizing Montai's App Ecosystem with R Shiny
item.description:
Context

By mid-2024, Montai’s internal web app landscape had fragmented. Different engineers built tools in their preferred frameworks — Python Streamlit, Python Dash, R Shiny, Jupyter notebooks — creating a sprawling ecosystem with inconsistent UX and duplicated effort. For a small data team (~5-6 people), this fragmentation imposed hidden costs: context-switching overhead, maintenance burden, and harder onboarding.

Facts:

  • 2024: Growing need for internal web apps (compound selection, data visualization, report generation)
  • Problem: Different engineers using different frameworks (Python Streamlit/Dash, R Shiny, Jupyter notebooks)
  • Pain: Inconsistent UX, duplicated effort, hard to maintain, context-switching cost
  • Stakes: Small team (~5-6 people) needed velocity + consistency

Technical leadership was needed to converge on a single approach. The challenge: balance team skills, deployment infrastructure, and use case requirements — while avoiding the trap of “one size fits all” dogma that ignores practical constraints.

item.title: A Biologist Caught What No Automated System Did
item.description:
Context

Two ML model configurations — one trained on a full compound dataset, one trained on a filtered subset — were producing nearly identical predictions. A biologist noticed. There were no automated data quality checks at any stage of the pipeline. No assertions, no monitoring, no alerting. The only reason this failure surfaced at all was that a domain expert recognized a pattern that didn’t make scientific sense.

That’s the starting condition: a failure that was invisible to every system we’d built, caught by someone paying attention.

item.title: Operating Discipline in Compound Nominations
item.description:
Context

Each nomination wave locked $30K–$100K of procurement and months of wet-lab capacity. A sloppy compound picklist translated directly into sunk experimental runs with no way to learn from the outcome.

The company’s computational drug discovery group could produce ranked compound lists on demand, yet every screening cycle restarted from zero. Scientists ran their own filters, mixed vendor catalogs with local enumerations, and argued from memory because knowledge lived in a system or in someone’s head. Assay readouts landed six months later with no mechanism to attribute success or failure back to the initial decision. The consequence: we could not learn whether structural diversity constraints mattered, whether AI rankings were overfitting, or whether procurement dollars were allocated to the right exploration vs. exploitation balance.

item.title: Scaling AI-Driven Drug Nominations from 250 to 7,000 Compounds
item.description:
Context

Early 2023 presented a defining challenge: Montai’s AI models could predict activity across millions of compounds, but manual library creation processes were bottlenecked at ~250 compounds per program. The central question wasn’t whether AI could generate predictions — it was whether we could build a scalable system that maintained scientific rigor while expanding the search space 20×.

Facts:

  • Baseline: 100’s of compounds, chosen manually for screening from within existing library
  • Stakes: Scale 10× to 100× per program to enabled by bioactivity ML models
  • Environment: Early-stage biotech, unproven concept
  • My role: First data science/product hire, architected pipeline

The challenge

How do you architect a multi-objective decision system, that provides an optimal starting point for drug discovery funnels, is understandable by all the key decision-makers at the organization?
